summary:
The Surrender of Athens (404 BC) ended the [[event:1010|Peloponnesian War]] and Athens's period of imperial greatness. After the destruction of its fleet at [[event:1013|Battle of Aegospotami]], Athens was blockaded by sea and its grain supply cut off. The population faced starvation. After months of siege with no prospect of relief, Athens surrendered to Lysander and the Spartan alliance.

The terms imposed were humiliating: the Long Walls connecting Athens to its port at Piraeus were demolished to music, reportedly played by Spartan flute-girls; the fleet was reduced to twelve ships; all overseas possessions were surrendered; and Athens was required to follow Spartan foreign policy. Corinth and Thebes reportedly called for the total destruction of the city and enslavement of its population — Sparta refused, unwilling to extinguish a city that had helped save Greece from Persia. The [[event:1017|Thirty Tyrants of Athens]] were installed as a puppet oligarchic government. Though Athens would eventually recover its democracy and resume a significant cultural role, it never again wielded the imperial power of the fifth century.
